ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crab white bee hoon. FOODON_03315091 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Crustaceans and products thereof) is used as the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for crab bee hoon as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for crab white bee hoon or equivalent composite seafood noodle dish.

serving.description: Shared portion for 2–3 persons (approximately 350 g per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving of crab white bee hoon for a single diner is approximately 350 g, representing one-third of a dish served for 2–3 persons at a restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Bee Hoon, cooked with seafood — adapted composite estimate using HPB Singapore Foodo (Fried Bee Hoon with Seafood) and USDA FoodData Central crab (blue crab, cooked, moist heat, NDB 15143) with milky broth adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for crab white bee hoon. Values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for seafood bee hoon (cooked rice vermicelli with seafood, ~130 kcal/100 g) with USDA cooked crab data (97 kcal/100 g, protein 20.5 g, fat 1.7 g) and adjusting for the milky white broth (evaporated milk contribution). The dish is approximately 40% rice vermicelli, 30% crab meat and shell, 25% milky broth, 5% aromatics. Weighted composite per 100 g of edible portion: energy ~118 kcal, protein ~9.5 g, fat ~3.8 g, carbohydrate ~11.5 g. Sodium elevated due to seasoning and stock. All values are estimates; a laboratory analysis is recommended for a production-quality record.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to crab white bee hoon composite dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crab white bee hoon. Rice vermicelli (bee hoon) has a published GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database. The milky broth and crab protein content are expected to moderate glycemic response further. GI of 53 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate for the composite dish. GL per serving = (53 × 40.25 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 21.3, rounded to 21.9 accounting for broth carbohydrate contribution. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1470 mg per 350 g serving) from seasoned stock, soy sauce, and salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute over 60% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~21.9 per serving) driven by rice vermicelli carbohydrate content, partially offset by crab protein and milky broth
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the rice vermicelli component contributes a moderate glycemic load; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and limiting the broth intake can help attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from evaporated milk and lard; crab provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content is the primary concern; the dish can be consumed occasionally in reduced portions, leveraging the cardiovascular benefit of crab's omega-3 content while requesting low-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab White Bee Hoon (螃蟹白米粉) is a celebrated Singaporean Chinese seafood dish featuring whole fresh crab cooked in a rich, milky-white broth served over soft rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The dish is a signature offering of restaurants such as House of White Bee Hoon and is typically served as a shared dish for 2–3 diners. The white broth is made by simmering chicken stock with evaporated milk, garlic, ginger, and white pepper, producing a creamy, savoury, and mildly sweet flavour profile. The crab — commonly Sri Lankan or mud crab — is braised directly in the broth, imparting a deep umami sweetness. The rice vermicelli absorbs the broth and becomes silky and flavourful. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density (~118 kcal/100 g), high-quality lean protein from crab (~9.5 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from rice vermicelli (~11.5 g/100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), making it a relatively moderate-GI option among noodle dishes. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to seasoned stock, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with crustacean shellfish or dairy allergies. It is not halal-certified at most establishments due to the use of lard in some preparations. Crab provides notable amounts of vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus, supporting neurological and immune function.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for crab white bee hoon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of HPB Foodo seafood bee hoon data and USDA FoodData Central cooked crab data, adjusted for the milky broth component. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli entry. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach; laboratory analysis would be required for high confidence.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. A laboratory proximate analysis is recommended for production use. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo seafood bee hoon reference entry. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources used. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources used. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources used. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in the source ingredients (rice vermicelli and crab are naturally free of industrial trans fats). 7. The dish is described as 'FOR 2-3 PAX' in the source record; serving size of 350 g per person is estimated based on a total dish weight of approximately 900–1050 g divided by 2.5 persons. 8. Halal status is uncertain; some preparations use lard — is_halal is set to false as a conservative default. 9. GI of 53 is a conservative upper-bound analogous estimate from rice vermicelli; actual GI of the composite dish may be lower due to protein and fat content moderating glycemic response. 10. Sodium estimate of 420 mg/100 g is based on typical restaurant-style seasoned seafood noodle broth; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant.
